learning_objectives: Perform integer division, compute remainders, and apply the division algorithm
Identify and verify properties of prime and composite numbers
Apply divisibility rules and the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
Connect number theory concepts to their role in cryptographic systems
readings: Katz and Lindell, Introduction to Modern Cryptography, Ch. 1 (mathematical background)
Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Ch. 4.1-4.2
Paar and Pelzl, Understanding Cryptography, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: The mathematics underlying modern cryptography is primarily number theory, the branch of mathematics concerned with the properties of integers. These concepts are not merely theoretical curiosities; they directly determine the security strength of the encryption systems that protect every internet transaction, every secure communication, and every digital signature. RSA, the most widely deployed public-key cryptosystem, relies on the difficulty of factoring large integers. Diffie-Hellman key exchange relies on the difficulty of computing discrete logarithms. Elliptic curve cryptography combines algebraic geometry with number theory to achieve the same security with smaller keys. Even symmetric ciphers like AES use number theory in their design, operating in finite fields (Galois fields) for their S-box and column-mixing operations. This course builds the mathematical foundations that security professionals need to understand, evaluate, and in some cases implement cryptographic systems. We begin with the most basic number theory concepts: divisibility, primes, and the structure of the integers. These foundations seem elementary, but they lead directly to the modular arithmetic and computational problems that make modern cryptography possible. By the end of this course, you will understand not just how to use cryptographic tools but why they work and what mathematical assumptions their security depends on.
key_concepts: Division algorithm: for any integers a and d > 0, there exist unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that a = d*q + r with 0 <= r < d
Divisibility: a divides b (written a | b) if there exists an integer k such that b = a*k; foundational for understanding factors and primes
Prime numbers: an integer p > 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and p itself; the atoms of number theory
Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: every integer greater than 1 has a unique prime factorization (up to ordering); e.g., 84 = 2^2 * 3 * 7
Composite numbers: integers with divisors other than 1 and themselves; can be factored into primes
Sieve of Eratosthenes: efficient algorithm for finding all primes up to a given limit; demonstrates the distribution of primes
Prime Number Theorem: the number of primes up to N is approximately N / ln(N); primes become sparser but never run out
Infinitude of primes: Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many primes (proof by contradiction: if p_1, ..., p_n were all primes, then p_1*p_2*...*p_n + 1 would be divisible by none of them)
Cryptographic relevance: RSA security depends on the difficulty of factoring n = p*q where p and q are large primes; generating these primes and verifying primality are essential operations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement the Sieve of Eratosthenes in Python to find all primes up to 10,000. Visualize the distribution of primes and compare with the Prime Number Theorem approximation N/ln(N). Then demonstrate how RSA key generation starts by selecting two large random primes (using Python's secrets module for cryptographic randomness) and testing primality with the Miller-Rabin test, connecting the abstract number theory to practical cryptographic implementation.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with sympy library for number theory functions

review.value: Every integer > 1 has a unique prime factorization. This means factoring is well-defined but not necessarily efficient. For small numbers, trial division works. For large numbers (hundreds of digits), the best known algorithms require sub-exponential but super-polynomial time. This computational asymmetry is the foundation of RSA.

Among 1024-bit numbers, approximately 1 in 710 is prime (by the Prime Number Theorem, about 1 in ln(2^1024) = 710). RSA key generation randomly selects 1024-bit odd numbers and tests primality until a prime is found. On average, about 355 random odd numbers must be tested.

Public-key cryptography needs mathematical operations that are easy in one direction and hard in reverse. Multiplying two 512-digit primes takes microseconds. Factoring their 1024-digit product takes longer than the age of the universe with current algorithms and computers. This one-way property is what makes RSA possible.

Determining whether a number is prime (primality testing) is efficient: the AKS algorithm runs in polynomial time, and probabilistic tests like Miller-Rabin are fast in practice. But finding the factors of a composite number (factoring) is believed to be hard. These are fundamentally different problems with very different computational complexity.

learning_objectives: Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, and exponentiation in modular arithmetic
Apply modular reduction to simplify computations with large numbers
Solve linear congruences and understand when solutions exist
Connect modular arithmetic operations to their use in cryptographic algorithms
readings: Rosen, Ch. 4.1-4.3
Katz and Lindell, Ch. 9.1-9.2 (number-theoretic foundations)
Paar and Pelzl, Ch. 6.1-6.2

lecture_notes.introduction: Modular arithmetic is the arithmetic of remainders: all operations are performed within a finite set {0, 1, 2, ..., n-1}, and results that exceed n-1 are reduced by taking the remainder when divided by n. This may seem like a mathematical curiosity, but it is the mathematical foundation of virtually all public-key cryptography. RSA encryption computes c = m^e mod n. Diffie-Hellman computes g^a mod p. Digital signatures, key exchange, and certificate validation all depend on modular arithmetic being efficient to compute (forward) but hard to reverse (backward) for certain operations. Modular arithmetic has special properties that make it suitable for cryptography. It wraps around: adding 1 to n-1 gives 0, creating a cyclic structure. It preserves algebraic structure: the standard algebraic rules (commutativity, associativity, distributivity) still hold. Most importantly, modular exponentiation can be computed efficiently even for enormous numbers using the square-and-multiply algorithm, while the reverse operation (the discrete logarithm: given g^a mod p, find a) is computationally infeasible for properly chosen parameters. This session establishes fluency in modular arithmetic operations, the congruence notation, and the connection to cryptographic computations.
key_concepts: Congruence notation: a is congruent to b mod n (written a = b (mod n)) if n divides (a - b); equivalently, a and b have the same remainder when divided by n
Modular addition: (a + b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n; results stay within {0, 1, ..., n-1}
Modular multiplication: (a * b) mod n = ((a mod n) * (b mod n)) mod n; intermediate results can be reduced at each step
Modular exponentiation: a^k mod n computed efficiently by the square-and-multiply algorithm (repeated squaring); avoids computing the enormous number a^k directly
Square-and-multiply algorithm: to compute a^k mod n, express k in binary, then square for each bit and multiply when the bit is 1; complexity is O(log k) multiplications
Linear congruence: ax = b (mod n) has a solution if and only if gcd(a, n) divides b; when solvable, there are gcd(a, n) solutions modulo n
Residue classes: the set Z_n = {0, 1, ..., n-1} with modular addition and multiplication forms a ring; when n is prime, Z_p is a field (every nonzero element has an inverse)

Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT): if n = n_1 * n_2 with gcd(n_1, n_2) = 1, then for any a_1, a_2, there is a unique x mod n such that x = a_1 (mod n_1) and x = a_2 (mod n_2); used in RSA for faster decryption
Clock arithmetic analogy: modular arithmetic mod 12 is the arithmetic of clock hours; 10 + 5 = 3 (mod 12) because 15 mod 12 = 3
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement modular exponentiation using the square-and-multiply algorithm in Python. Compute 7^256 mod 13 both by the naive method (computing 7^256 first, then reducing) and by square-and-multiply (reducing at each step). Show that square-and-multiply is feasible for 2048-bit numbers while naive computation is impossible. Then demonstrate the Chinese Remainder Theorem by solving a system of congruences and showing how CRT accelerates RSA decryption.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with sympy for modular arithmetic verification
Timing comparison scripts for naive vs efficient modular exponentiation

review.value: RSA encryption: c = m^e mod n. RSA decryption: m = c^d mod n. Both operations use modular exponentiation on numbers with 2048+ bits. The square-and-multiply algorithm makes this feasible: computing 2048-bit modular exponentiation requires about 2048 modular multiplications, each of which is fast.

Without modular reduction, 7^256 is a number with 217 digits. With mod 13, the result is a single digit. Cryptographic computations involve exponents with hundreds of digits; without intermediate modular reduction, the numbers would have more digits than atoms in the universe.

When p is prime, every nonzero element of Z_p has a multiplicative inverse. This means division (except by zero) is always possible in Z_p. This algebraic structure is essential for cryptography: RSA requires inverses mod phi(n), and ECC operates in fields Z_p.

The Chinese Remainder Theorem lets RSA decryption work with two smaller modular exponentiations (mod p and mod q) instead of one large one (mod n = p*q). Since modular exponentiation cost is cubic in the number of bits, two half-size computations are about 4 times faster than one full-size computation.

learning_objectives: Apply probabilistic primality testing algorithms (Fermat, Miller-Rabin)
Understand the computational complexity of integer factorization
Evaluate the security implications of factoring advances for RSA
Generate cryptographically suitable prime numbers
readings: Katz and Lindell, Ch. 9.2 (primality testing and factoring)
Paar and Pelzl, Ch. 7.7-7.8
NIST SP 800-57 key size recommendations

lecture_notes.introduction: The security of RSA rests on the assumption that factoring the product of two large primes is computationally infeasible. The RSA modulus n = p * q is public, but recovering p and q from n should require more computation than any attacker can perform. This assumption has held for nearly 50 years, but it is not proven: we do not know for certain that no efficient factoring algorithm exists. What we do know is that the best known classical factoring algorithms (General Number Field Sieve) have sub-exponential but super-polynomial running time, and the largest RSA modulus factored to date is RSA-250 (829 bits), achieved in 2020 using approximately 2700 CPU-core-years. RSA-2048 (2048 bits) is estimated to require approximately 10^14 times more computation, placing it well beyond current capabilities. However, Shor's quantum algorithm factors integers in polynomial time on a quantum computer, which would break RSA entirely once sufficiently large quantum computers exist. On the other side of the factoring problem is primality testing: efficiently determining whether a given number is prime without factoring it. The Miller-Rabin probabilistic primality test is used in practice for generating RSA primes, providing extremely high confidence (error probability < 2^(-128) with 64 iterations) in polynomial time. This session covers both sides: primality testing for key generation and factoring resistance for security.
key_concepts: Fermat's Little Theorem: if p is prime and gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^(p-1) = 1 (mod p); basis for Fermat primality test
Fermat primality test: if a^(n-1) != 1 (mod n) for some a, then n is composite; if it equals 1, n is probably prime (but Carmichael numbers fool this test)
Carmichael numbers: composite numbers where a^(n-1) = 1 (mod n) for all a coprime to n; rare but exist (561, 1105, ...); the Fermat test cannot detect them
Miller-Rabin primality test: stronger version that checks additional conditions beyond Fermat; detects Carmichael numbers; error probability < 1/4 per round, so k rounds give error < (1/4)^k

Prime generation for RSA: generate a random odd number of the desired bit length, test with Miller-Rabin (64 rounds), if composite, increment by 2 and repeat; average attempts: about ln(2^n)/2 for n-bit primes
Trial division: divide by all primes up to sqrt(n); guaranteed correct but impractical for large n; O(sqrt(n)) complexity
General Number Field Sieve (GNFS): fastest known classical factoring algorithm; complexity approximately exp(1.9 * (ln n)^(1/3) * (ln ln n)^(2/3)); sub-exponential
RSA factoring records: RSA-768 (768 bits) factored in 2009 using ~1500 CPU-years; RSA-250 (829 bits) factored in 2020 using ~2700 CPU-core-years; RSA-2048 remains infeasible
Shor's algorithm: factors integers in polynomial time O((log n)^3) on a quantum computer; would break all RSA key sizes; motivates post-quantum cryptography research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement the Miller-Rabin primality test in Python from scratch. Test it against known primes and composites, including Carmichael numbers (561, 1105, 1729). Generate a 1024-bit RSA prime by repeated random selection and Miller-Rabin testing, counting the number of candidates tested. Estimate the security of RSA-2048 by extrapolating from published factoring records using the GNFS complexity formula.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with the secrets module for cryptographic random number generation
Table of RSA factoring records for reference

review.value: With 64 rounds of Miller-Rabin, the probability of accepting a composite as prime is less than (1/4)^64 = 2^(-128), which is less than the probability of hardware failure during the computation. This is why Miller-Rabin is considered sufficient for cryptographic prime generation despite being probabilistic.

GNFS complexity for n-bit modulus is approximately exp(1.9 * n^(1/3) * (log n)^(2/3)). Doubling the bit length from 1024 to 2048 increases the factoring cost by a factor of approximately 10^14. This is why 2048-bit RSA is considered safe against classical computers for the foreseeable future.

Shor's algorithm factors an n-bit number using O(n^3) quantum operations. A 2048-bit RSA modulus would require approximately 4000 logical qubits. Current quantum computers have ~1000 noisy qubits, far from the millions of physical qubits needed for error correction. Estimates for breaking RSA-2048: 2030-2040, but highly uncertain.

For some protocols (Diffie-Hellman), safe primes p where (p-1)/2 is also prime are preferred. They ensure the multiplicative group Z_p* has a large prime-order subgroup, preventing small-subgroup attacks. Safe primes are rarer (about 1 in ln(p)^2), making generation slower.

learning_objectives: Compute the GCD using the Euclidean algorithm
Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm to find Bezout coefficients
Compute modular multiplicative inverses using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm
Apply modular inverses to RSA key generation
readings: Rosen, Ch. 4.3-4.4
Katz and Lindell, Ch. 9.1
Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch. 31.2

lecture_notes.introduction: The Greatest Common Divisor and the Extended Euclidean Algorithm are among the most ancient and most useful algorithms in mathematics, dating back to Euclid around 300 BCE. The Euclidean algorithm computes gcd(a, b) by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of dividing the two, converging to the GCD in at most O(log(min(a,b))) steps. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm goes further: it finds integers x and y such that ax + by = gcd(a, b) (Bezout's identity). When gcd(a, n) = 1, this gives x such that ax = 1 (mod n), meaning x is the modular multiplicative inverse of a modulo n. Modular inverses are essential for cryptography. In RSA, the private exponent d is the modular inverse of the public exponent e modulo phi(n): d = e^(-1) mod phi(n), computed by the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. Without modular inverses, RSA key generation would be impossible. In Diffie-Hellman, modular inverses are used in various protocol variants. In elliptic curve arithmetic, point addition requires computing modular inverses of coordinate differences. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm is the standard method for computing these inverses, and it is one of the few algorithms that every security professional should be able to implement from memory.
key_concepts: GCD definition: gcd(a, b) is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b; gcd(a, 0) = a; gcd(0, 0) is undefined (or conventionally 0)
Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b); repeat until the remainder is 0; the last nonzero remainder is the GCD
Algorithm efficiency: the Euclidean algorithm requires at most O(log(min(a, b))) steps; each step reduces the smaller number by at least half
Bezout's identity: for any integers a, b, there exist integers x, y such that ax + by = gcd(a, b); the Extended Euclidean Algorithm finds x and y
Extended Euclidean Algorithm: extends the basic algorithm by tracking the linear combination at each step; returns gcd(a, b) and coefficients x, y
Modular inverse: a^(-1) mod n exists if and only if gcd(a, n) = 1; computed as the x from ax + ny = 1 (since ax = 1 - ny = 1 (mod n))
RSA key generation: choose primes p, q; compute n = p*q and phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1); choose e coprime to phi(n); compute d = e^(-1) mod phi(n) using Extended Euclidean
Coprime integers: a and b are coprime if gcd(a, b) = 1; every nonzero element of Z_p (p prime) is coprime to p, so every nonzero element has an inverse
Euler's totient function: phi(n) = count of integers in {1, ..., n} coprime to n; phi(p) = p-1 for prime p; phi(p*q) = (p-1)(q-1) for distinct primes p, q
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement the Extended Euclidean Algorithm in Python from scratch. Use it to compute gcd(240, 46) = 2 and find x, y such that 240x + 46y = 2. Then compute the modular inverse of 17 mod 3120 (the RSA example from week 2): 17^(-1) mod 3120 = 2753, and verify: 17 * 2753 mod 3120 = 1. Finally, complete a full RSA key generation: choose p = 61, q = 53, compute n = 3233, phi(n) = 3120, choose e = 17, compute d = 2753, encrypt m = 65 as c = 65^17 mod 3233 = 2790, decrypt c = 2790^2753 mod 3233 = 65.

review.value: The RSA private exponent d = e^(-1) mod phi(n). This requires gcd(e, phi(n)) = 1 (e and phi(n) must be coprime). The standard choice e = 65537 = 2^16 + 1 is prime and coprime to most phi(n) values. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm computes d in O(log(phi(n))) steps.

For 2048-bit RSA, phi(n) has about 2048 bits. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm requires about 2048 iterations, each involving division of numbers with up to 2048 bits. This is extremely fast (microseconds) compared to the modular exponentiations in encryption and decryption.

Before computing an inverse, verify gcd(a, n) = 1. If gcd(a, n) > 1, the inverse does not exist. In RSA, if gcd(e, phi(n)) > 1, choose a different e. The standard e = 65537 rarely fails this check, but it must always be verified.

learning_objectives: Define and compute probabilities using sample spaces and events
Apply counting principles (multiplication rule, permutations, combinations) to security scenarios
Calculate the probability of brute-force attack success for various key spaces
Model security events using probability distributions
readings: Katz and Lindell, Ch. 3.1-3.3 (computational security definitions)
Rosen, Ch. 7.1-7.3
Anderson, Security Engineering, Ch. 5 (probability in security)

lecture_notes.introduction: Probability is the language of security analysis. Every security assessment ultimately answers a probabilistic question: what is the probability that an attacker succeeds? Key strength is measured in bits of security, where n bits means an attacker must perform 2^n operations on average. Password entropy quantifies the probability that a random guess succeeds. False positive and false negative rates determine whether a security system helps or hinders. Birthday attacks exploit the counter-intuitive probability of collisions. Understanding probability enables you to make quantitative security decisions rather than relying on vague assertions. Should your password be 8 characters or 12? How large should your hash output be to resist collision attacks? How many authentication attempts should be allowed before lockout? These questions all have precise probabilistic answers. This session introduces the probability concepts most relevant to security analysis, with every example drawn from real security scenarios. We cover the sample space model, counting principles for computing key space sizes, and the probability distributions that model attacker success in brute-force, dictionary, and statistical attacks.
key_concepts: Sample space: the set of all possible outcomes; for a 128-bit key, the sample space is {0, 1}^128 with 2^128 elements
Events and probability: P(event) = |favorable outcomes| / |total outcomes| for uniform distributions; P(guessing a 128-bit key) = 1 / 2^128
Multiplication rule: if process A has m outcomes and process B has n outcomes, the combined process has m * n outcomes; a 4-digit PIN has 10^4 = 10,000 possibilities
Permutations: ordered arrangements; P(n, k) = n! / (n-k)!; relevant for ordered password character selection without repetition
Combinations: unordered selections; C(n, k) = n! / (k!(n-k)!); relevant for selecting features in anomaly detection
Key space size: the number of possible keys determines brute-force difficulty; 128-bit key = 2^128 possibilities = 3.4 x 10^38
Expected attempts for brute-force: on average, an attacker must try half the key space to find the correct key; expected attempts = 2^(n-1) for n-bit key
Geometric distribution: models the number of trials until first success; P(X = k) = (1-p)^(k-1) * p; models brute-force attack progress
Password entropy: H = log2(possible_passwords); a password chosen uniformly from a set of N possibilities has entropy log2(N) bits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate the key space sizes and brute-force resistance for various security parameters: a 4-digit PIN (10^4 = 10,000 possibilities, ~13 bits of entropy), an 8-character lowercase password (26^8 = ~208 billion, ~37 bits), a 12-character mixed-case alphanumeric password (62^12 = ~3.2 x 10^21, ~71 bits), and a 256-bit AES key (2^256 = ~1.2 x 10^77). Estimate the time to brute-force each at different attack speeds (1 million guesses/sec, 1 billion guesses/sec, GPU cluster at 10 billion guesses/sec).
setup_requirements: Scientific calculator for large number computation

review.value: A system has n bits of security if an attacker needs 2^n operations to break it. AES-128 provides 128 bits of security against brute force. RSA-2048 provides approximately 112 bits of security (because factoring is faster than brute force). The security level determines the difficulty of the best known attack.

Entropy = log2(possible_passwords). An 8-character password from 26 lowercase letters: log2(26^8) = 37.6 bits. At 10 billion guesses/sec (GPU cluster), exhausting the space takes 26^8 / 10^10 = 20.8 seconds. This is why short, simple passwords are insecure regardless of the hashing algorithm.

The expected number of brute-force attempts is |keyspace|/2, but the worst case is |keyspace|. For security analysis, use the expected value for average-case estimates and the full keyspace for worst-case guarantees. An attacker might get lucky and succeed on the first try.

2^128 = 3.4 x 10^38. If every atom on Earth (approximately 10^50) were a computer performing 10^12 operations per second, it would take 3.4 x 10^38 / (10^50 * 10^12) = 3.4 x 10^(-24) seconds to exhaust a 128-bit key space. Wait, that is too fast. Recalculate: 10^50 * 10^12 = 10^62 ops/sec total. 3.4 x 10^38 / 10^62 = 3.4 x 10^(-24) seconds. This means 128 bits is actually not enough against such a hypothetical adversary, but no real adversary has 10^50 computers.

learning_objectives: Compute conditional probabilities using the definition P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B)
Apply Bayes' theorem to update threat probabilities with new evidence
Analyze the base rate fallacy in security alert systems
Calculate false positive and false negative rates for security detection systems
readings: Rosen, Ch. 7.3 (Bayes' theorem)
Anderson, Security Engineering, Ch. 5 (base rate fallacy)
Axelsson, The Base-Rate Fallacy and the Difficulty of Intrusion Detection

lecture_notes.introduction: Conditional probability and Bayes' theorem are essential tools for security analysis because security decisions are almost always made under uncertainty with incomplete information. When an intrusion detection system raises an alert, what is the probability that a real attack is occurring? When a user fails three login attempts, what is the probability that it is an attacker versus a legitimate user who forgot their password? When a security scan finds a potential vulnerability, what is the probability it is a true positive? Bayes' theorem provides the framework for answering these questions by combining prior probabilities (base rates) with new evidence (observations) to produce updated probabilities (posterior beliefs). The base rate fallacy is one of the most important concepts in practical security: even a highly accurate detection system produces overwhelming numbers of false positives when the base rate of actual attacks is very low. If an IDS has a 99% true positive rate and a 1% false positive rate, and only 0.01% of traffic is malicious, then 99% of alerts will be false positives. Understanding this mathematically prevents wasting resources chasing false alarms and enables proper tuning of detection thresholds. This session develops the mathematical tools for probabilistic reasoning about security events.
key_concepts: Conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B); the probability of A given that B has occurred; updates our belief about A when we observe B
Independence: A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A); observing B gives no information about A; key streams in cryptography must be independent of plaintext
Bayes' theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A) * P(A) / P(B); converts P(evidence|hypothesis) into P(hypothesis|evidence)
Prior, likelihood, and posterior: P(attack) is the prior (base rate); P(alert|attack) is the likelihood (detection rate); P(attack|alert) is the posterior (what we want to know)
Base rate fallacy: ignoring the prior probability when interpreting evidence; if attacks are rare, even accurate detectors produce many false positives
True positive rate (sensitivity): P(alert|attack) = probability of detecting a real attack; higher is better
False positive rate: P(alert|no attack) = probability of alerting when there is no attack; lower is better
Precision: P(attack|alert) = fraction of alerts that are true positives; depends heavily on the base rate
ROC curve: plotting true positive rate vs false positive rate for different detection thresholds; the curve shows the trade-off between catching attacks and generating false alarms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate the precision of an IDS with 99% detection rate and 1% false positive rate under three scenarios: (1) 50% of traffic is malicious (precision = 99%), (2) 1% of traffic is malicious (precision = 50%), (3) 0.01% of traffic is malicious (precision = 0.98%). Show how precision drops dramatically as the base rate decreases. Then apply Bayes' theorem to a threat intelligence scenario: given a threat intelligence report with 80% accuracy, update the probability that a specific IP is malicious from the prior 0.1% (base rate) to the posterior using the report as evidence.

review.value: An IDS with 99% detection rate and 1% false positive rate on a network where 0.01% of connections are malicious: P(attack|alert) = (0.99 * 0.0001) / (0.99 * 0.0001 + 0.01 * 0.9999) = 0.000099 / 0.010098 = 0.98%. Only about 1 in 100 alerts is a real attack. The other 99 are false positives.

If a threat intel feed says IP X is malicious (feed accuracy 80%), and the base rate of malicious IPs is 0.1%: P(malicious|flagged) = (0.8 * 0.001) / (0.8 * 0.001 + 0.2 * 0.999) = 0.0008 / 0.2006 = 0.4%. Even with an 80% accurate feed, only 0.4% of flagged IPs are truly malicious because the base rate is so low.

Every detection system has a threshold that trades false positives for false negatives. A low threshold catches more attacks (high sensitivity) but generates more false alerts. A high threshold reduces false alerts but misses more attacks. The ROC curve visualizes this trade-off, and the area under the curve (AUC) measures overall discriminative ability.

Security Operations Centers implicitly use Bayesian reasoning: a single IDS alert has low prior probability of being a real attack. But when that alert correlates with a second indicator (unusual login time), the posterior probability increases. Each additional correlated indicator further updates the probability, justifying escalation.

learning_objectives: Compute descriptive statistics and build statistical baselines for normal network behavior
Apply hypothesis testing to determine whether observed behavior is anomalous
Use z-scores and percentiles for threshold-based anomaly detection
Evaluate detection systems using Type I and Type II error rates
readings: Bejtlich, The Practice of Network Security Monitoring, Ch. 7
Chandola et al., Anomaly Detection: A Survey (key sections)
NIST SP 800-94 Guide to IDS and IPS

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistical anomaly detection identifies security threats by recognizing deviations from established baselines of normal behavior. Unlike signature-based detection, which can only find known threats, statistical detection can identify novel attacks that have never been seen before, as long as they cause measurable deviations from normal patterns. The approach requires building statistical models of normal behavior from historical data, then flagging observations that fall outside expected ranges. Network traffic volumes, connection counts, data transfer sizes, login frequencies, and API call patterns all follow distributions that can be characterized statistically. When a user who normally transfers 50 MB per day suddenly transfers 5 GB, or when a server that normally handles 100 connections per hour suddenly receives 10,000, the statistical deviation flags the anomaly. The mathematical foundation involves descriptive statistics for baseline building, hypothesis testing for determining whether a deviation is statistically significant, and the careful management of false positive and false negative rates. This session develops the statistical tools needed to build, evaluate, and tune anomaly detection systems.
key_concepts: Descriptive statistics for baselines: mean (average behavior), median (robust to outliers), standard deviation (spread of normal variation), and percentiles (defining normal ranges)
Normal distribution: many aggregated metrics approximate a bell curve; the 68-95-99.7 rule gives quick thresholds: values beyond 3 standard deviations occur less than 0.3% of the time
Z-score anomaly detection: z = (x - mean) / std_dev; flag observations where |z| exceeds a threshold (commonly z > 3); simple, interpretable, but assumes normal distribution
Hypothesis testing framework: null hypothesis H0 (behavior is normal) vs alternative H1 (behavior is anomalous); significance level alpha determines the false positive rate
Type I error (false positive): rejecting H0 when it is true; concluding anomaly when behavior is normal; wastes analyst time and causes alert fatigue
Type II error (false negative): failing to reject H0 when it is false; missing a real anomaly; allows attacks to go undetected
Trade-off: lowering the threshold catches more anomalies (fewer Type II errors) but generates more false alerts (more Type I errors); the optimal balance depends on the cost of each error type

Non-parametric methods: when data does not follow a normal distribution (network traffic is often heavy-tailed), use percentile-based thresholds or the interquartile range (IQR) method instead of z-scores
Time series baselines: accounting for daily, weekly, and seasonal patterns in baseline construction; Monday morning traffic differs from Sunday midnight traffic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a statistical anomaly detection system for network traffic. Load 30 days of hourly traffic volume data, compute mean, standard deviation, and percentiles for each hour of each day of the week (separate baselines for Monday 9 AM, Monday 10 AM, etc.). Apply z-score detection with threshold z > 3 to a new week of data containing one simulated data exfiltration event (2 AM spike). Show the detection triggering on the anomalous spike. Then intentionally lower the threshold to z > 2 and show the increase in false positives. Plot the ROC curve.
setup_requirements: Python with numpy, scipy, and pandas for statistical computation
Network traffic dataset (simulated or real) with labeled anomalies

review.value: z = (x - mean) / std_dev. A value with z = 4.5 is 4.5 standard deviations from the mean, occurring by chance with probability approximately 3.4 x 10^(-6), or about once every 294,000 observations. This is highly anomalous and warrants investigation. However, with millions of observations per day, even rare events occur frequently.

Network traffic has strong daily and weekly patterns: high during business hours, low at night, reduced on weekends. A single global baseline would flag all nighttime traffic as anomalous (low) and miss anomalies during peak hours. Build separate baselines for each time period to account for these patterns.

Network traffic, file sizes, and web request sizes follow heavy-tailed distributions (long right tails) rather than normal distributions. Z-scores assume normality and will produce too many false positives for heavy-tailed data. Use percentile-based thresholds (flag if > 99.9th percentile) or log-transform the data before computing z-scores.

If a system generates 1000 alerts per day and 99% are false positives, analysts process 10 real alerts and 990 false ones. After months of this, analysts stop investigating alerts carefully and miss real attacks. Reducing false positives is as important as improving detection rates.

learning_objectives: Apply set operations (union, intersection, complement, difference) to security policy analysis
Construct and simplify Boolean expressions for firewall rules and access control policies
Use truth tables and Boolean algebra laws to verify security policy equivalences
Connect set theory and Boolean algebra to packet filtering and policy optimization
readings: Rosen, Ch. 2.1-2.3 (set theory) and Ch. 12.1-12.3 (Boolean algebra)
Cheswick et al., Firewalls and Internet Security, Ch. 3-4
Boolean algebra reference for digital logic

lecture_notes.introduction: Set theory and Boolean algebra provide the mathematical language for expressing, analyzing, and optimizing security policies. Every firewall rule is a Boolean expression: accept the packet if (source IP is in set A) AND (destination port is in set B) AND (protocol is TCP). Access control lists combine set membership tests with Boolean operators. Network segmentation defines zones as sets of IP addresses with rules governing traffic between zones. Boolean algebra allows us to simplify complex policy rules, detect redundant rules, identify conflicting rules, and verify that a policy achieves its intended behavior. Set theory is equally important for security analysis beyond network policies. Threat modeling defines asset sets, threat actor sets, and vulnerability sets, then analyzes their intersections. Vulnerability scanners compare the set of installed software versions against the set of known vulnerable versions. Compliance frameworks define required control sets, and audits verify that the implemented control set is a superset. Understanding these mathematical foundations enables precise reasoning about security configurations that informal analysis might miss. A firewall policy with hundreds of rules may contain redundant rules, shadowed rules (rules that never match because a preceding rule matches first), and conflicting rules (rules that would both accept and deny the same packet). Boolean algebra provides the tools to analyze and resolve these issues.
key_concepts: Set notation and operations: union (A or B), intersection (A and B), complement (not A), difference (A but not B), symmetric difference (A or B but not both)
Set membership: x in A tests whether element x belongs to set A; fundamental operation for firewall rules (is this source IP in the allowed set?)
Boolean algebra: a mathematical system with two values (true/false or 1/0) and three operations (AND, OR, NOT) satisfying specific axioms
Boolean algebra laws: identity (A AND 1 = A), domination (A AND 0 = 0), idempotent (A AND A = A), complement (A AND NOT A = 0), De Morgan's (NOT(A AND B) = NOT A OR NOT B)
Firewall rules as Boolean expressions: ACCEPT if (src in 10.0.0.0/8) AND (dst_port in {80, 443}) AND (protocol = TCP); each clause is a set membership test
Policy optimization: using Boolean algebra to simplify rules; combining (src = 10.0.1.0/24 OR src = 10.0.2.0/24) into src = 10.0.0.0/22 when appropriate
Rule shadowing: rule R2 is shadowed by rule R1 if every packet matching R2 also matches R1 and R1 appears first; R2 never fires
Rule conflicts: two rules that match the same packet but specify different actions (accept vs deny); resolved by rule ordering (first match wins)
Venn diagrams for security analysis: visualizing the intersection of user roles, resource permissions, and network zones
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model a firewall policy with 10 rules as Boolean expressions. Use Boolean algebra to identify two redundant rules and one shadowed rule. Simplify the policy from 10 rules to 7 rules while maintaining identical behavior. Verify the equivalence by constructing truth tables for key packet combinations. Then analyze an access control matrix using set operations: show that the set of users who can read AND write to a resource is the intersection of the read permission set and the write permission set.

review.value: NOT (allow SSH AND allow HTTP) = deny SSH OR deny HTTP. De Morgan's laws are essential for converting between allow-list and deny-list representations of security policies. An 'allow everything except SSH and HTTP' policy is equivalent to 'deny SSH or deny HTTP', which can be expressed as two deny rules.

Firewalls process rules top-to-bottom, first match wins. If rule 1 allows all traffic from 10.0.0.0/8 and rule 5 denies traffic from 10.0.1.5/32, the deny rule never fires because the allow rule matches first. This is a shadowing problem. Move the more specific deny rule before the broader allow rule.

Threat = Assets intersection Threats intersection Vulnerabilities. If any set in the intersection is empty, the threat does not exist. Removing an asset, mitigating a vulnerability, or blocking a threat actor reduces the intersection to empty for that specific threat scenario.

Karnaugh maps and Quine-McCluskey algorithm minimize Boolean expressions to the smallest equivalent form. Applied to firewall policies, this reduces the number of rules while maintaining identical behavior, improving both readability and processing speed.

learning_objectives: Calculate Shannon entropy for probability distributions and message sources
Apply entropy to measure password strength, key randomness, and data compressibility
Understand perfect secrecy and the one-time pad through information theory
Analyze the information leakage of encryption schemes
readings: Katz and Lindell, Ch. 2 (perfectly secret encryption)
Cover and Thomas, Elements of Information Theory, Ch. 1-2
Shannon, Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems (selected sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Information theory, founded by Claude Shannon in 1948, provides the mathematical framework for quantifying information, uncertainty, and secrecy. Shannon entropy H(X) = -sum(p(x) * log2(p(x))) measures the average amount of uncertainty (or information content) in a random variable. For a uniform distribution over 2^n outcomes, the entropy is exactly n bits, which matches our intuitive notion of key strength. For a non-uniform distribution (like English text or human-chosen passwords), the entropy is less than the maximum, reflecting the patterns and predictability in the source. Information theory directly connects to security in several fundamental ways. Perfect secrecy, achieved by the one-time pad, means the ciphertext provides zero information about the plaintext: H(plaintext | ciphertext) = H(plaintext). This is the theoretical gold standard, but it requires a key as long as the message. Practical cryptosystems settle for computational security: the ciphertext does reveal some information in theory, but extracting it requires infeasible computation. Entropy also measures the quality of random number generators (a good generator has maximum entropy), the strength of passwords (human-chosen passwords have much less entropy than random strings), and the compressibility of data (lossless compression cannot reduce data below its entropy).
key_concepts: Shannon entropy: H(X) = -sum(p(x) * log2(p(x))) for each outcome x; measured in bits; represents the average number of yes/no questions needed to identify the outcome
Maximum entropy: H(X) = log2(n) for a uniform distribution over n outcomes; any non-uniformity reduces entropy; maximum entropy = maximum uncertainty = maximum security
Entropy of English: approximately 1.0-1.5 bits per character (vs 4.7 bits per character for uniform random from 26 letters); the difference reflects the predictability of English

Password entropy: calculated from the actual distribution of human-chosen passwords, not from the character set size; '123456' contributes very little entropy despite being 6 characters from a 10-digit alphabet

Perfect secrecy: H(M|C) = H(M); the ciphertext provides zero information about the message; achieved if and only if the key space is at least as large as the message space and each key is used exactly once
One-time pad: plaintext XOR random key = ciphertext; achieves perfect secrecy; the key must be truly random, at least as long as the message, used only once, and securely shared
Joint entropy: H(X, Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X); the total information in two variables is the information in X plus the additional information in Y given X
Mutual information: I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X); measures how much knowing Y reduces uncertainty about X; for perfect secrecy, I(M; C) = 0

Entropy in random number generation: a truly random bit source has entropy of 1 bit per bit; a biased source has less; health tests for random number generators check that entropy meets minimum thresholds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate the entropy of various probability distributions: a fair coin (1 bit), a loaded coin with P(H) = 0.9 (0.47 bits), a uniform 8-character lowercase password (37.6 bits), and a dictionary word from a 50,000-word dictionary (15.6 bits). Show that human-chosen passwords have much less entropy than their character-set-size calculation would suggest by analyzing a leaked password database. Demonstrate the one-time pad: encrypt and decrypt a message, then show that the same ciphertext could decrypt to any plaintext of the same length, proving perfect secrecy.
setup_requirements: Leaked password frequency list for analysis

review.value: A 128-bit AES key chosen uniformly at random has 128 bits of entropy: the maximum for a 128-bit string. A 128-bit key generated by a flawed RNG that outputs only even numbers has 127 bits of entropy. If the RNG has a bias that an attacker can exploit, the effective entropy may be much lower, directly reducing security.

An 8-character random password from 72 characters has 49.4 bits of entropy. But a human-chosen 8-character password averages about 18-28 bits of entropy because humans use dictionary words, predictable patterns (capital first letter, digit at end), and common substitutions. This is why password policies based on character requirements fail.

The OTP achieves perfect secrecy but is impractical for most applications: the key must be truly random (not pseudorandom), as long as the message (no compression), used exactly once (reuse destroys security completely), and securely distributed (the key distribution problem). These constraints limit OTP use to the most sensitive communications.

Lossless compression cannot reduce data below its entropy. If English text has ~1.3 bits per character and is stored as 8-bit ASCII, the maximum compression ratio is about 8/1.3 = 6:1. If encrypted data appears to have maximum entropy (good cipher), it cannot be compressed at all. Compressibility of ciphertext indicates a weak cipher.

learning_objectives: Perform arithmetic in GF(2^8) as used in AES S-boxes and MixColumns
Understand permutation and substitution operations mathematically
Analyze the confusion and diffusion properties that make ciphers secure
Compute the avalanche effect quantitatively
readings: Paar and Pelzl, Understanding Cryptography, Ch. 4 (AES)
Daemen and Rijmen, The Design of Rijndael, Ch. 2-4 (mathematical background)
NIST FIPS 197: AES Standard

lecture_notes.introduction: Symmetric encryption algorithms like AES are built on precise mathematical structures that give them their security properties. AES operates in the Galois field GF(2^8), a finite field with 256 elements where addition is XOR and multiplication is polynomial multiplication modulo an irreducible polynomial. The AES S-box computes the multiplicative inverse in GF(2^8) followed by an affine transformation, creating a highly nonlinear substitution that is the primary source of AES's resistance to linear and differential cryptanalysis. The MixColumns operation multiplies each column of the state by a fixed matrix over GF(2^8), providing the diffusion that ensures each output byte depends on all input bytes after two rounds. Understanding these mathematical operations reveals why AES is secure and what properties a symmetric cipher must have. Shannon identified two essential properties: confusion (making the relationship between key and ciphertext complex) and diffusion (spreading the influence of each plaintext bit across the entire ciphertext). The S-box provides confusion through its nonlinear GF(2^8) operations, and the ShiftRows plus MixColumns provide diffusion through linear mixing. The avalanche effect, where changing one input bit changes approximately half the output bits, is the measurable consequence of good confusion and diffusion. This session covers the mathematics that makes symmetric cryptography work.
key_concepts: Galois field GF(2^8): 256 elements represented as polynomials of degree <= 7 with binary coefficients; addition is XOR (coefficient-wise mod 2); multiplication is polynomial multiplication mod the irreducible polynomial x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1
GF(2^8) addition: XOR of the byte representations; 0x57 XOR 0x83 = 0xD4; equivalent to adding polynomials mod 2
GF(2^8) multiplication: polynomial multiplication modulo the irreducible polynomial; more complex than addition; lookup tables or the xtime() function are used in practice

AES S-box: computes the multiplicative inverse in GF(2^8) (with 0 mapping to 0), then applies an affine transformation; produces a highly nonlinear mapping that resists linear and differential attacks
Permutations: bijective functions that rearrange elements; AES ShiftRows is a byte-level permutation of the state matrix rows; permutations alone do not provide security (they are linear)
Substitution vs permutation: substitution (S-box) provides nonlinearity (confusion); permutation (ShiftRows) and linear mixing (MixColumns) provide diffusion; both are needed
Shannon's confusion and diffusion: confusion means each ciphertext bit depends on multiple key bits in a complex way; diffusion means each ciphertext bit depends on multiple plaintext bits
Avalanche effect: changing one bit of the input should change approximately 50% of the output bits; measured as the Hamming distance between outputs for inputs differing in one bit
MixColumns as matrix multiplication: each column of the AES state is multiplied by a fixed 4x4 matrix over GF(2^8); this linear operation spreads each byte's influence across the column
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement GF(2^8) addition and multiplication in Python. Show that GF(2^8) addition is XOR by example. Implement the xtime() function for efficient GF(2^8) multiplication. Compute the AES S-box value for a single byte by finding the GF(2^8) inverse and applying the affine transformation. Demonstrate the avalanche effect: encrypt two plaintexts differing by one bit using AES and count the number of differing bits in the ciphertexts (should be approximately 64 out of 128).
setup_requirements: AES S-box lookup table for verification
PyCryptodome for AES encryption in avalanche demonstration

review.value: GF(2^8) is a field with 256 elements. Every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse. Addition is XOR (fast). Multiplication requires polynomial reduction (more complex). AES uses this field because: 256 elements fit in one byte, field arithmetic provides algebraic structure for security proofs, and the inverse operation creates the desired nonlinearity.

The multiplicative inverse in GF(2^8) has the highest possible nonlinearity for a function from GF(2^8) to itself. This means it provides the maximum resistance to linear approximation (used in linear cryptanalysis). The AES designers chose it specifically for this mathematical property.

A function satisfies the strict avalanche criterion if changing any single input bit causes each output bit to flip with probability exactly 1/2. AES achieves this after 2 rounds. After all 10/12/14 rounds, the avalanche is essentially perfect: each output bit appears random with respect to each input bit.

Linear operations (XOR, bit shifts, permutations) alone cannot provide security because they can be described by simple equations an attacker can solve. Nonlinear operations (S-boxes) create complex dependencies that prevent algebraic attacks. AES alternates nonlinear (SubBytes) and linear (ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey) operations.

learning_objectives: Derive the mathematical correctness of RSA encryption and decryption using Euler's theorem
Implement RSA key generation, encryption, and decryption from mathematical primitives
Analyze the mathematical security of RSA: factoring, chosen-ciphertext attacks, and padding
Evaluate RSA key size recommendations based on factoring difficulty
readings: Katz and Lindell, Ch. 13.1-13.2 (RSA)
Paar and Pelzl, Ch. 7 (RSA and the modular inverse)
Boneh, Twenty Years of Attacks on the RSA Cryptosystem

lecture_notes.introduction: RSA is the most widely deployed public-key cryptosystem, used in TLS certificates, SSH keys, email encryption, digital signatures, and code signing. Its security and correctness rest entirely on number theory. The correctness of RSA, meaning that decryption recovers the original plaintext, follows from Euler's theorem: a^(phi(n)) = 1 (mod n) for gcd(a, n) = 1. Since the public and private exponents satisfy e * d = 1 (mod phi(n)), we have m^(ed) = m^(1 + k*phi(n)) = m * (m^(phi(n)))^k = m * 1^k = m (mod n). The security of RSA rests on the computational difficulty of factoring n = p * q. If an attacker can factor n, they can compute phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) and then compute d = e^(-1) mod phi(n), exactly as the legitimate key generator did. Without knowing the factorization, computing d from the public key (n, e) is believed to be as hard as factoring n. This session covers the complete mathematical treatment of RSA: the number theory that makes it work, the security reductions that relate its strength to factoring, the mathematical attacks that constrain implementation choices, and the padding schemes (OAEP) that address the mathematical weaknesses of textbook RSA.
key_concepts: Euler's theorem: a^(phi(n)) = 1 (mod n) when gcd(a, n) = 1; the foundation of RSA's correctness
Euler's totient function: phi(n) counts integers in {1, ..., n-1} coprime to n; phi(p*q) = (p-1)(q-1) for distinct primes p, q
RSA key generation: choose primes p, q; compute n = p*q; compute phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1); choose e coprime to phi(n); compute d = e^(-1) mod phi(n); public key = (n, e), private key = (n, d)
RSA encryption: c = m^e mod n; RSA decryption: m = c^d mod n; correctness: m^(ed) mod n = m (by Euler's theorem)
Textbook RSA weaknesses: deterministic (same plaintext always produces the same ciphertext), multiplicative homomorphism (E(m1) * E(m2) = E(m1 * m2)), and vulnerable to small-exponent attacks
RSA-OAEP: Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding adds randomness and structure before encryption, making RSA probabilistic and CCA-secure
Security reduction: breaking RSA is no harder than factoring n; it is unknown whether it is easier (the RSA problem may be easier than factoring, but this is not believed to be the case)
Common RSA attacks: small public exponent (e = 3 with small messages), common modulus attack, Wiener's attack on small d, Hastad's broadcast attack, Bleichenbacher's PKCS#1 v1.5 padding oracle
Key size recommendations: NIST recommends RSA-2048 (112 bits of security) through 2030; RSA-3072 (128 bits) for longer-term security; RSA-4096 for the most conservative applications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement RSA from mathematical primitives in Python (no crypto library). Generate two 512-bit primes using Miller-Rabin, compute n and phi(n), choose e = 65537, compute d using Extended Euclidean, encrypt a message, and decrypt to recover it. Then demonstrate a textbook RSA attack: encrypt the same message twice and show the ciphertext is identical (deterministic), then multiply two ciphertexts and show the product decrypts to the product of the plaintexts (homomorphism). Finally, show how RSA-OAEP padding prevents both attacks.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with the implementations from previous weeks (Miller-Rabin, Extended Euclidean, modular exponentiation)

review.value: We need m^(ed) = m (mod n). Since ed = 1 + k*phi(n): m^(ed) = m^(1 + k*phi(n)) = m * (m^(phi(n)))^k. By Euler's theorem, m^(phi(n)) = 1 (mod n) when gcd(m, n) = 1. So m^(ed) = m * 1^k = m (mod n). The proof extends to all m (including gcd(m, n) > 1) using the Chinese Remainder Theorem.

65537 = 2^16 + 1 is a Fermat prime. Its binary representation has only two 1-bits, making modular exponentiation with this exponent require only 17 multiplications (16 squarings and 1 multiplication). This makes encryption fast. It is also large enough to prevent small-exponent attacks.

Textbook RSA (no padding) is insecure: it is deterministic (leaks whether the same message was sent twice), malleable (attacker can manipulate ciphertext to change the plaintext), and vulnerable to chosen-ciphertext attacks. Always use RSA-OAEP for encryption or RSA-PSS for signatures.

The RSA problem (computing m from c = m^e mod n given only the public key) is no harder than factoring (if you can factor n, you can solve the RSA problem). Whether the RSA problem is strictly easier than factoring is unknown. Most cryptographers believe they are equivalent in difficulty, but this has not been proven.

learning_objectives: Define elliptic curves and understand the group law for point addition
Perform point addition and scalar multiplication on elliptic curves over finite fields
Compare ECC security and efficiency with RSA at equivalent security levels
Understand the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)
readings: Paar and Pelzl, Understanding Cryptography, Ch. 9
Hankerson et al., Guide to Elliptic Curve Cryptography, Ch. 1-3
NIST FIPS 186-5: Digital Signature Standard (ECDSA sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Elliptic Curve Cryptography achieves the same security level as RSA with dramatically smaller key sizes. A 256-bit ECC key provides approximately 128 bits of security, equivalent to a 3072-bit RSA key. This 12:1 ratio in key size translates to faster computation, less bandwidth, and reduced storage, making ECC the preferred choice for resource-constrained environments like mobile devices, IoT, and high-performance servers. ECC is based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. An elliptic curve over a prime field Z_p is defined by the equation y^2 = x^3 + ax + b (mod p), and the points on this curve form an abelian group under a geometrically motivated addition operation. Scalar multiplication (computing n*P = P + P + ... + P, n times) is efficient, but the reverse operation (given P and Q = n*P, find n) is the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem, which is believed to be intractable for properly chosen curves. Unlike integer factorization where sub-exponential algorithms (GNFS) exist, the best known algorithm for ECDLP is fully exponential (Pollard's rho), which is why ECC achieves equivalent security with much smaller keys. This session introduces the mathematics of elliptic curves, the group law, scalar multiplication, and the security comparison with RSA.
key_concepts: Elliptic curve equation: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b over Z_p where 4a^3 + 27b^2 != 0 (mod p) ensures no singular points
Points on the curve: the set of (x, y) pairs satisfying the equation plus the point at infinity O (the identity element)
Point addition (P != Q): the line through P and Q intersects the curve at a third point R'; reflect R' over the x-axis to get P + Q; computed algebraically using the slope formula
Point doubling (P = Q): use the tangent line at P instead of the secant; the slope is (3x^2 + a) / (2y) mod p
Point at infinity: O serves as the identity element; P + O = O + P = P for all points P
Additive inverse: -P = (x, -y mod p); P + (-P) = O
Scalar multiplication: n*P = P + P + ... + P (n times); computed efficiently using the double-and-add algorithm (analogous to square-and-multiply for modular exponentiation)
ECDLP: given points P and Q = n*P, finding the integer n; believed to require O(sqrt(p)) operations (Pollard's rho); this is exponential in the key size
ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman): Alice sends a*G, Bob sends b*G, shared secret is a*b*G; the ECDLP prevents an eavesdropper from computing a or b from a*G or b*G
Standard curves: NIST P-256, P-384, P-521; Curve25519 (designed by Bernstein for efficiency and security); Ed25519 for signatures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement point addition and scalar multiplication on a small elliptic curve y^2 = x^3 + 2x + 3 over Z_97. Find all points on the curve, verify the group law, and compute scalar multiples. Then implement ECDH key exchange using the small curve. Compare the computation time and key sizes for ECC-256, RSA-2048, and RSA-3072 to demonstrate ECC's efficiency advantage.
setup_requirements: Python 3 for elliptic curve arithmetic implementation
PyCryptodome or cryptography library for standard curve comparison

review.value: The best algorithm for ECDLP (Pollard's rho) runs in O(sqrt(n)) = O(2^(k/2)) for k-bit keys. The best algorithm for factoring (GNFS) runs in sub-exponential time. This means ECC needs only k/2 bits of security from a k-bit key, while RSA needs much larger keys. Result: 256-bit ECC = 3072-bit RSA = 128 bits of security.

Over the reals, the line through two curve points intersects the curve at exactly one more point (by Bezout's theorem for cubics). We negate the y-coordinate of this third point to get the sum. Over finite fields, the same algebraic formula works even though the geometric intuition breaks down. The key insight is that the group law is defined algebraically, not geometrically.

Not all curves are secure. The curve parameters (a, b, p) must be chosen to avoid known attacks: anomalous curves (where the curve order equals p), supersingular curves (where p divides the trace of Frobenius), and curves with smooth order (where Pohlig-Hellman reduces the ECDLP). Standard curves (P-256, Curve25519) have been vetted for these weaknesses.

Shor's algorithm breaks ECC just as it breaks RSA. A quantum computer can solve ECDLP in polynomial time. ECC key sizes provide no additional protection against quantum attacks. Post-quantum cryptography uses lattice-based, hash-based, or code-based problems instead of ECDLP.

learning_objectives: Define the three security properties of cryptographic hash functions: pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, and collision resistance
Apply the birthday paradox to determine the collision resistance of hash functions
Understand the Merkle-Damgard construction and length extension attacks
Evaluate hash function security levels for SHA-256, SHA-3, and BLAKE3
readings: Katz and Lindell, Ch. 6 (hash functions)
Paar and Pelzl, Ch. 11
NIST FIPS 180-4 (SHA-2) and FIPS 202 (SHA-3)

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptographic hash functions map arbitrary-length inputs to fixed-length outputs with three security properties that make them foundational to modern security. Pre-image resistance means that given a hash value h, finding any message m such that H(m) = h is computationally infeasible. Second pre-image resistance means that given a message m1, finding a different message m2 such that H(m1) = H(m2) is infeasible. Collision resistance means finding any two distinct messages m1 and m2 such that H(m1) = H(m2) is infeasible. The birthday paradox is the key mathematical insight for understanding collision resistance. For a hash function with n-bit output, a brute-force pre-image attack requires approximately 2^n operations, but finding a collision requires only approximately 2^(n/2) operations due to the birthday paradox. This is why SHA-256 (256-bit output) provides 128 bits of collision resistance, not 256 bits. The birthday paradox is not a paradox but a counter-intuitive probability result: in a group of 23 people, there is a greater than 50% chance that two share a birthday. This same mathematical phenomenon determines the collision resistance of every hash function. Understanding the mathematics ensures you choose appropriate hash output sizes for your security requirements.
key_concepts: Hash function definition: H: {0,1}* -> {0,1}^n; maps arbitrary-length input to fixed-length n-bit output; deterministic and efficiently computable
Pre-image resistance: given h = H(m), finding m requires approximately 2^n operations (brute force); prevents recovering passwords from hashes
Second pre-image resistance: given m1, finding m2 != m1 with H(m1) = H(m2) requires approximately 2^n operations; prevents substituting a different document with the same hash
Collision resistance: finding any m1 != m2 with H(m1) = H(m2) requires approximately 2^(n/2) operations (birthday attack); the weakest of the three properties

Birthday paradox: in a set of k randomly chosen elements from a space of N, the probability of a collision is approximately 1 - e^(-k^2/(2N)); 50% collision probability occurs around k = 1.2 * sqrt(N)

Birthday attack on hashes: for an n-bit hash, compute approximately 2^(n/2) hashes and look for a collision; for SHA-256, this is 2^128 operations (infeasible); for MD5 (128-bit), it is 2^64 operations (feasible)

Merkle-Damgard construction: processes the message in blocks, chaining the output of each compression through an internal state; used by SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512; vulnerable to length extension attacks
Length extension attack: given H(m) for unknown m, an attacker can compute H(m || padding || m') for arbitrary m' without knowing m; affects Merkle-Damgard hashes; prevented by HMAC or SHA-3

SHA-3 (Keccak): uses sponge construction instead of Merkle-Damgard; immune to length extension attacks; internal state (1600 bits) is much larger than the output; provides all three security properties at their theoretical maximum
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demonstrate the birthday paradox experimentally: generate random 16-bit hashes and find a collision after approximately 2^8 = 256 attempts (versus 2^16 = 65536 for a pre-image). Plot the number of hashes computed vs the probability of having found a collision, comparing the experimental results with the theoretical prediction. Then demonstrate a length extension attack on SHA-256: given H(secret || message), compute H(secret || message || padding || attacker_data) without knowing the secret, showing why HMAC uses a different construction.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with hashlib for hash computation
Length extension attack demonstration (hlextend library or manual implementation)

review.value: For a 256-bit hash, finding a collision requires approximately 2^128 operations. For a 128-bit hash, only 2^64 operations (feasible with modern hardware). This is why MD5 (128-bit) has practical collision attacks but SHA-256 (256-bit) does not. Always choose hash output at least 2x your desired security level.

MD5 collisions can be found in seconds on a laptop (Xiaoyun Wang, 2004). SHA-1 collisions were demonstrated by Google in 2017 (SHAttered attack). Both are considered insecure for collision resistance. SHA-1 is still used in legacy systems but should be replaced with SHA-256 or SHA-3.

If an API authenticates messages with tag = SHA256(secret || message), an attacker who knows the tag and message (but not the secret) can compute a valid tag for message || padding || attacker_data. This is because Merkle-Damgard's final state can be used as the starting state for additional data. HMAC prevents this by using H(K XOR opad || H(K XOR ipad || message)).

SHA-256: standard choice, 128 bits of collision resistance, Merkle-Damgard (use HMAC for authentication). SHA-3 (Keccak): sponge construction, no length extension, good for hardware. BLAKE3: extremely fast, tree-structured for parallelism, 128 bits of security. MD5/SHA-1: insecure, use only for non-security purposes (checksums).

learning_objectives: Distinguish between true random, pseudorandom, and cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generators
Evaluate the security of PRNGs using statistical tests and entropy assessment
Understand how CSPRNG failures have led to real-world security breaches
Apply proper random number generation practices for cryptographic key generation
readings: Katz and Lindell, Ch. 3.3-3.4 (pseudorandom generators)
NIST SP 800-90A (DRBG recommendation)
Debian OpenSSL PRNG vulnerability analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Random number generation is the unsung foundation of all cryptographic security. Every cryptographic key, every initialization vector, every nonce, and every challenge in an authentication protocol depends on random numbers being truly unpredictable. If an attacker can predict the random numbers used in key generation, they can recover the key. If they can predict the nonce in a digital signature, they can extract the private key. The history of cryptographic failures includes many cases where the cryptographic algorithms were mathematically perfect but the random number generator was flawed. The 2008 Debian OpenSSL bug reduced the entropy of generated keys to approximately 15 bits (only 32,768 possible keys), making all SSL certificates and SSH keys generated on affected systems trivially breakable. The 2010 PlayStation 3 signing key was extracted because Sony used the same random number for every ECDSA signature. The Dual EC DRBG backdoor allegedly allowed the NSA to predict the output of a NIST-recommended random number generator. These examples demonstrate that random number generation is not a theoretical concern but a practical security requirement that demands careful implementation. This session covers the mathematics of random number generation, the distinction between statistical randomness and cryptographic security, and the proper use of entropy sources and CSPRNGs in security applications.
key_concepts: True Random Number Generators (TRNGs): harvest entropy from physical processes (thermal noise, radioactive decay, timing jitter); produce genuinely unpredictable bits; limited throughput

Pseudorandom Number Generators (PRNGs): deterministic algorithms that produce statistically random-looking output from a seed; output is completely determined by the seed; not suitable for cryptography

Cryptographically Secure PRNGs (CSPRNGs): PRNGs where predicting future output from past output is computationally infeasible, even knowing the algorithm; examples: /dev/urandom, CryptGenRandom, HMAC-DRBG
Entropy: the measure of unpredictability in a random source; a CSPRNG needs at least 128 bits of true entropy in its seed to produce cryptographically secure output
Entropy sources in computers: hardware RNG (Intel RDRAND, ARM TRNG), interrupt timing jitter, disk seek times, mouse movements, network packet timing; combined and conditioned before use
DRBG (Deterministic Random Bit Generator): NIST SP 800-90A specifies three approved DRBGs: HMAC-DRBG, CTR-DRBG, and Hash-DRBG; all require seeding from an entropy source

Statistical randomness tests: NIST SP 800-22 test suite includes frequency test, runs test, serial test, approximate entropy test, and others; passing tests is necessary but not sufficient for cryptographic security

Debian OpenSSL bug: a code change in 2006 removed most entropy from the PRNG seed, reducing the key space to about 32,768 possible keys for 2 years before discovery; all keys generated during this period were compromised

Proper key generation: use OS-provided CSPRNG (secrets module in Python, /dev/urandom on Linux, CryptGenRandom on Windows); never use math.random(), rand(), or similar non-cryptographic PRNGs for security
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare the output of Python's random module (Mersenne Twister, not cryptographically secure) with the secrets module (CSPRNG). Show that the Mersenne Twister's state can be reconstructed from 624 consecutive outputs, allowing prediction of all future outputs. Then demonstrate the NIST SP 800-22 statistical test suite on both outputs (both pass), showing that statistical tests alone cannot distinguish a weak PRNG from a CSPRNG. Simulate the Debian OpenSSL vulnerability: generate RSA keys using only 15 bits of entropy and show that all possible keys can be enumerated in seconds.
setup_requirements: Python 3 with random and secrets modules
NIST SP 800-22 statistical test suite (or Python implementation)

review.value: Python's random.random() uses the Mersenne Twister: fast, statistically good, but its internal state can be recovered from 624 outputs, making all future outputs predictable. Python's secrets.token_bytes() uses the OS CSPRNG: slower, but output is unpredictable even to an attacker who has seen all previous outputs. Always use secrets for anything security-related.

A CSPRNG with 128 bits of entropy in its seed can produce gigabytes of cryptographically secure output. The entropy is in the seed, not in the output. Statistical tests measure output quality but cannot measure the entropy of the seed. A perfectly seeded CSPRNG and a badly seeded CSPRNG produce output that looks identical statistically.

Debian OpenSSL (2008): 15 bits of entropy, all keys breakable. PlayStation 3 (2010): static random number in ECDSA signatures, private key extracted. Dual EC DRBG: potential NSA backdoor in a NIST standard. These are not theoretical attacks; they caused real security breaches affecting millions of systems.

For cryptographic keys: use secrets.token_bytes(32) in Python, or /dev/urandom on Linux. For nonces: use a counter or random value from CSPRNG, never reuse. For IVs: use random value from CSPRNG, different for each encryption. Never seed a PRNG with the current time (predictable), process ID (limited range), or any other low-entropy source.

learning_objectives: Apply combinatorial counting to enumerate attack surfaces and key spaces
Compute the probability of guessing attacks using combinatorial analysis
Analyze password policies and authentication systems using combinatorics
Evaluate the combinatorial complexity of cryptographic attacks
readings: Rosen, Ch. 6.1-6.5 (counting principles)
Katz and Lindell, relevant sections on security parameters
Florencio and Herley, A Large-Scale Study of Web Password Habits (password statistics)

lecture_notes.introduction: Combinatorics, the mathematics of counting, is essential for security analysis because security fundamentally depends on the size of spaces that attackers must search. How many possible passwords satisfy a given policy? How many possible keys does an algorithm have? How many attempts does an attacker need to find a collision, a pre-image, or a valid authentication token? Combinatorial analysis provides precise answers to these questions, enabling quantitative security assessment rather than qualitative guesswork. This final session brings together all the mathematical tools from the course into a comprehensive framework for security analysis. We use number theory to understand cryptographic algorithms, probability to model attacker success, information theory to measure randomness and leakage, and combinatorics to count possibilities and compute attack complexities. The session applies these tools to analyze real security systems: password policies, multi-factor authentication, key derivation functions, token-based authentication, and certificate transparency. By combining all the mathematical disciplines covered in this course, you will be able to perform the quantitative security analysis that distinguishes rigorous security engineering from security theater.
key_concepts: Multiplication principle: if a process has k independent steps with n_1, n_2, ..., n_k choices per step, the total number of outcomes is n_1 * n_2 * ... * n_k
Permutations with repetition: choosing r items from n with replacement and order mattering gives n^r outcomes; an r-character password from an n-character alphabet has n^r possibilities
Combinations: choosing r items from n without replacement and order not mattering gives C(n,r) = n! / (r!(n-r)!) outcomes; relevant for selecting subset attacks

Password policy analysis: a policy requiring 8-12 characters from lowercase (26), uppercase (26), digits (10), and at least one from each category; the actual count is less than 62^12 due to the constraints

Multi-factor authentication combinatorics: if factor 1 has N_1 possibilities and factor 2 has N_2 possibilities and they are independent, brute-forcing both requires N_1 * N_2 attempts; independence is crucial

Token space analysis: a 6-digit TOTP code has 10^6 = 1,000,000 possibilities per 30-second window; with rate limiting (3 attempts per window), the probability of guessing correctly is 3/1,000,000 = 0.0003%
Birthday attack combinatorics: the number of pairs among k items is C(k, 2) = k(k-1)/2; the probability of a collision grows quadratically with the number of attempts, not linearly

Pigeonhole principle: if more than N items are placed in N containers, at least one container has more than one item; applied to hash functions: 2^256 + 1 distinct messages must produce at least one collision for SHA-256
Inclusion-exclusion principle: for counting elements in the union of sets while avoiding double-counting; useful for analyzing complex password policies with multiple requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze the security of a complete authentication system using combinatorics. Calculate: (1) the password space for a policy requiring 8+ characters with complexity requirements (using inclusion-exclusion to handle the 'at least one from each category' constraint), (2) the TOTP brute-force probability with rate limiting, (3) the combined MFA strength (password + TOTP), and (4) the expected time for an attacker to compromise the system under various attack models (online brute force, offline with stolen hash, social engineering). Compare with NIST SP 800-63 requirements for authentication assurance levels.
setup_requirements: NIST SP 800-63 authentication assurance level reference

review.value: A policy requiring at least one uppercase, one lowercase, one digit, and one symbol from 8 characters: the total space is not simply 72^8. By inclusion-exclusion, subtract passwords missing each category: 72^8 - C(4,1)*62^8 + C(4,2)*52^8 - C(4,3)*42^8 + ... This gives approximately 3.0 x 10^14, less than 72^8 = 7.2 x 10^14. But human behavior reduces effective entropy far more than this mathematical reduction.

If a password has 2^40 possibilities and a TOTP has 10^6, the combined brute force space is 2^40 * 10^6 = approximately 2^60. An attacker who steals the password hash and brute-forces the password (2^40) still needs the TOTP (10^6 online guesses, rate-limited to 3 per 30 seconds). MFA forces the attacker to defeat both factors, multiplying the attack cost.

Without rate limiting, brute-forcing a 6-digit code requires at most 10^6 attempts, achievable in seconds. With rate limiting (3 attempts per 30-second window), exhausting the code space requires 10^6/3 * 30 seconds = approximately 115 days. Rate limiting transforms a trivially breakable system into a practically secure one.

Number theory makes RSA and ECC possible. Modular arithmetic enables efficient encryption. Probability quantifies security levels. Statistics enables anomaly detection. Information theory measures randomness. Boolean algebra structures security policies. Combinatorics counts attack spaces. Every security decision has a mathematical foundation. Understanding the math enables rigorous security engineering.
