questions.question: A company's password policy requires 8-character passwords using lowercase (26), uppercase (26), digits (10), and special characters (10). Calculate the entropy of a randomly generated password under this policy. How long would a GPU cluster performing 10 billion hashes per second take to exhaust the keyspace? How does bcrypt at 4 hashes/second change this analysis?
questions.answer: Character set size = 26 + 26 + 10 + 10 = 72. Total possible passwords = 72^8 = 722,204,136,308,736 (approximately 7.22 x 10^14). Entropy = log2(72^8) = 8 * log2(72) = 8 * 6.17 = 49.4 bits. GPU cluster at 10 billion (10^10) hashes/second: Time to exhaust = 7.22 x 10^14 / 10^10 = 72,220 seconds = approximately 20 hours. Time to find on average (half the keyspace) = approximately 10 hours. This means SHA-256-hashed passwords with this policy are crackable in under a day with a GPU cluster. With bcrypt at 4 hashes/second per core (cost factor 12): Time to exhaust = 7.22 x 10^14 / 4 = 1.81 x 10^14 seconds = approximately 5.7 million years. Even with 1000 parallel cores: 5,700 years. bcrypt's key stretching makes brute force infeasible even for this moderate-entropy password. Analysis: The password policy provides 49.4 bits of entropy, which is insufficient against fast hash functions but adequate against slow key derivation functions like bcrypt. The takeaway: password security depends on both the entropy of the password and the computational cost of the hash function.

rubric.key: Correct entropy calculation
Correct time-to-exhaust for GPU cluster with SHA-256
Meaningful bcrypt comparison showing the transformative effect of key stretching

questions.question: Explain why the Miller-Rabin primality test is preferred over the Fermat test for generating RSA primes, and calculate the maximum probability of error when using Miller-Rabin with 64 rounds.
questions.answer: The Fermat primality test has a fundamental weakness: Carmichael numbers are composite numbers that pass the Fermat test for all bases coprime to them. The smallest Carmichael number is 561 = 3 * 11 * 17. For any base a with gcd(a, 561) = 1, a^560 = 1 (mod 561), so the Fermat test always says 'probably prime' despite 561 being composite. There are infinitely many Carmichael numbers, so the Fermat test can never provide certainty. The Miller-Rabin test adds an additional check: after computing a^d mod n (where n-1 = 2^s * d), it checks a sequence of squarings. For a true prime, the sequence must follow a specific pattern (the value must be 1 or n-1 at certain points). Carmichael numbers fail this additional check with high probability. Miller-Rabin correctly identifies 561 as composite. Error probability: Each round of Miller-Rabin has error probability at most 1/4 (it declares a composite number as 'probably prime' with probability at most 1/4). With 64 independent rounds, the error probability is at most (1/4)^64 = 2^(-128) = approximately 2.94 x 10^(-39). For comparison, the probability of a cosmic ray flipping a bit in your computer's memory during the computation is much higher. The 2^(-128) error bound is considered more than sufficient for cryptographic applications.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of Carmichael number weakness in Fermat test
Description of how Miller-Rabin overcomes this weakness
Correct error probability calculation with practical interpretation

questions.question: An intrusion detection system has a true positive rate (sensitivity) of 99.5% and a false positive rate of 0.1%. On this network, 0.001% of connections are actual attacks. Calculate P(attack | alert) using Bayes' theorem. Interpret the result and explain its implications for the SOC team.
questions.answer: Using Bayes' theorem: P(attack | alert) = P(alert | attack) * P(attack) / P(alert). P(alert | attack) = 0.995 (true positive rate). P(attack) = 0.00001 (base rate = 0.001%). P(no attack) = 0.99999. P(alert | no attack) = 0.001 (false positive rate = 0.1%). P(alert) = P(alert | attack) * P(attack) + P(alert | no attack) * P(no attack) = 0.995 * 0.00001 + 0.001 * 0.99999 = 0.00000995 + 0.00099999 = 0.00100994. P(attack | alert) = 0.00000995 / 0.00100994 = 0.00985 = approximately 0.99%. Interpretation: Despite the IDS having a 99.5% detection rate and only a 0.1% false positive rate, fewer than 1% of alerts represent real attacks. For every genuine attack alert, there are approximately 100 false positives. SOC implications: (1) The SOC team will experience severe alert fatigue, spending 99% of their investigation time on false positives. (2) The IDS should be tuned for higher precision, potentially accepting a lower detection rate in exchange for fewer false positives. (3) Alerts should be correlated with other indicators (multiple IDS rules, threat intelligence, behavioral analysis) to increase the posterior probability before escalation. (4) Automation should handle initial triage, escalating only alerts that pass multiple evidence checks. (5) The key insight: the extremely low base rate of attacks dominates the calculation despite the high-quality detector.

rubric.key: Correct Bayes' theorem calculation
Correct interpretation of the low precision despite high sensitivity
Practical SOC implications including alert fatigue and correlation strategies

questions.question: A security system uses a 6-digit numeric code that changes every 30 seconds (TOTP). An attacker can make at most 3 attempts per 30-second window before lockout. Calculate: (a) the probability of a successful guess in one window, (b) the expected number of windows to succeed, and (c) how rate limiting compares to no rate limiting.
questions.answer: (a) Total possible codes: 10^6 = 1,000,000. Attempts per window: 3. Probability of success in one window: 3/1,000,000 = 0.000003 = 0.0003%. (b) The number of windows to succeed follows a geometric distribution with p = 3/10^6. Expected number of windows = 1/p = 10^6/3 = 333,333 windows. At 30 seconds per window: 333,333 * 30 = 10,000,000 seconds = approximately 115.7 days. On average, the attacker needs about 116 days of continuous attempts to guess the code. However, the code changes every 30 seconds, so each window is independent, and the attacker never makes progress. (c) Without rate limiting, the attacker can make unlimited attempts per window. If they can attempt 100,000 codes per second (automated attack), they exhaust the 1,000,000-code space in 10 seconds, well within the 30-second window. Success probability per window approaches 100%. With rate limiting (3 attempts per window), the attacker's throughput drops to 3/30 = 0.1 attempts per second, and the expected time to success increases from ~10 seconds to ~116 days. Rate limiting provides a factor of approximately 10^6 increase in attack difficulty.

rubric.key: Correct probability calculation for one window
Correct expected time calculation using geometric distribution
Clear comparison of rate-limited vs unlimited showing the security multiplication factor

quizzes.topic: Statistics, Set Theory, and Information Theory

questions.question: A network IDS uses z-score anomaly detection. The baseline shows average daily outbound data transfer of 500 MB with a standard deviation of 100 MB. Today's transfer is 950 MB. Calculate the z-score, determine whether it triggers an alert at threshold z > 3, and discuss the limitations of this approach for network traffic data.
questions.answer: z = (x - mean) / std_dev = (950 - 500) / 100 = 450 / 100 = 4.5. Since z = 4.5 > 3 (threshold), this triggers an alert. Statistical significance: assuming a normal distribution, a z-score of 4.5 corresponds to a probability of approximately 3.4 x 10^(-6). This level of traffic occurs by chance about once every 294,000 days (approximately 805 years). This is statistically highly anomalous and warrants investigation as potential data exfiltration. Limitations of z-score detection for network traffic: (1) Network traffic is typically NOT normally distributed. It follows heavy-tailed distributions (log-normal or Pareto) where large values are much more common than a normal distribution predicts. Using z-scores with normal assumptions will produce excessive false positives for heavy-tailed data. (2) Traffic has strong temporal patterns: weekday vs weekend, business hours vs nights, end-of-month reporting spikes. A single global baseline ignores these patterns. The 950 MB might be normal for end-of-quarter financial reporting but anomalous on a regular Tuesday. (3) The mean and standard deviation are sensitive to outliers in the training data. A few large transfers in the baseline period can inflate the standard deviation, making future anomalies harder to detect. (4) A sophisticated attacker can slowly increase their data transfer rate over time, causing the baseline to shift and the exfiltration to appear normal. Better approaches: use time-of-day and day-of-week specific baselines, use percentile-based thresholds instead of z-scores, and apply log-transformation before computing z-scores to handle heavy tails.

rubric.key: Correct z-score calculation and alert determination
Statistical significance interpretation
Multiple well-explained limitations specific to network traffic

questions.question: Calculate the entropy of the following probability distribution over passwords: P('123456') = 0.10, P('password') = 0.08, P('qwerty') = 0.05, P(other) = 0.77 (uniform over 10 million other passwords). What does the entropy tell you about the security of this password distribution?
questions.answer: H(X) = -sum(p(x) * log2(p(x))). Top passwords: H_top = -(0.10 * log2(0.10) + 0.08 * log2(0.08) + 0.05 * log2(0.05)). log2(0.10) = -3.322, so -0.10 * (-3.322) = 0.3322. log2(0.08) = -3.644, so -0.08 * (-3.644) = 0.2915. log2(0.05) = -4.322, so -0.05 * (-4.322) = 0.2161. H_top = 0.3322 + 0.2915 + 0.2161 = 0.8398 bits. Remaining passwords: Each of the 10 million other passwords has probability 0.77 / 10,000,000 = 7.7 x 10^(-8). Each contributes: -7.7 x 10^(-8) * log2(7.7 x 10^(-8)) = -7.7 x 10^(-8) * (-23.63) = 1.82 x 10^(-6) bits. Total for all 10 million: 10^7 * 1.82 x 10^(-6) = 18.2 bits. H_other = -0.77 * log2(0.77) + 0.77 * log2(10^7) (conditional entropy approach). Actually, more precisely: H_other = sum over all 10M passwords of -(p_i * log2(p_i)). Each p_i = 0.77/10^7. H_other = 10^7 * (-(0.77/10^7) * log2(0.77/10^7)) = -0.77 * log2(0.77/10^7) = -0.77 * (log2(0.77) - log2(10^7)) = -0.77 * (-0.378 - 23.25) = -0.77 * (-23.63) = 18.20 bits. Total entropy: H = H_top + H_other = 0.84 + 18.20 = 19.04 bits. Security interpretation: The entropy is approximately 19 bits, meaning on average an attacker needs about 2^19 = 524,288 guesses with an optimal strategy (trying the most probable passwords first). This is extremely low security. Compared to a uniform distribution over 10 million passwords (entropy = log2(10^7) = 23.25 bits), the skewed distribution loses about 4 bits of entropy. An attacker trying just the top 3 passwords has a 23% chance of success. The effective security is determined by the weakest users: even if 77% of users choose from 10 million passwords, the 23% using common passwords compromise the system for targeted attacks.

rubric.key: Correct entropy calculation with proper handling of the mixed distribution
Meaningful security interpretation comparing with uniform distribution
Recognition that top passwords dominate attack strategy

questions.question: Explain why Shor's quantum algorithm threatens RSA and ECC but not AES, referencing the specific mathematical problems each relies on and the impact of quantum computing on each.
questions.answer: RSA relies on the hardness of integer factorization: given n = p*q, finding p and q. Shor's algorithm solves integer factorization in polynomial time O((log n)^3) on a quantum computer, completely breaking RSA regardless of key size. A 2048-bit RSA modulus would be factored in hours on a sufficiently large quantum computer. ECC relies on the hardness of the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem: given points P and Q = n*P, finding n. Shor's algorithm (adapted for discrete logarithms) also solves ECDLP in polynomial time, breaking ECC of all key sizes. A 256-bit ECC key would fall as easily as RSA-2048. AES is a symmetric cipher that does not rely on a number-theoretic hard problem. Its security comes from the difficulty of searching through the keyspace (brute force). Grover's quantum algorithm provides a quadratic speedup for unstructured search: it finds a target in a space of N elements using O(sqrt(N)) queries instead of O(N). For AES-256, Grover reduces the effective security from 256 bits to 128 bits (2^128 quantum operations). Since 2^128 operations remain computationally infeasible even for quantum computers, AES-256 is considered quantum-safe. AES-128 would be reduced to 64 bits of quantum security, which is marginal and may become feasible, so AES-256 is recommended. The fundamental difference: RSA and ECC rely on structured mathematical problems with efficient quantum solutions. AES relies on unstructured search with only a quadratic quantum speedup. This is why post-quantum research focuses on replacing RSA/ECC (asymmetric) while doubling key sizes for AES (symmetric).

rubric.key: Correct explanation of Shor's polynomial-time impact on RSA and ECC
Correct explanation of Grover's quadratic speedup impact on AES
Clear distinction between structured problems (RSA/ECC) and unstructured search (AES)

rubric.key: Correct n, phi(n), and d computation
Correct encryption using modular exponentiation
Correct decryption recovering m = 5

questions.question: Explain why textbook RSA (without padding) is insecure by describing two specific attacks, and explain how RSA-OAEP prevents each attack.
questions.answer: Attack 1: Deterministic encryption. Textbook RSA encrypts the same plaintext to the same ciphertext every time: c = m^e mod n is a deterministic function. An attacker who intercepts ciphertext c can test whether a guessed message m' is the plaintext by computing m'^e mod n and comparing with c. For messages from a small space (yes/no, buy/sell, specific dollar amounts), the attacker can enumerate all possible messages and find the match. Prevention by OAEP: OAEP adds random padding to the message before encryption. The padded message is m' = OAEP(m, random_padding), and different random padding produces different ciphertexts for the same message. The attacker cannot enumerate messages because each message has 2^256 possible padded forms (with 256 bits of randomness). Attack 2: Multiplicative homomorphism. Textbook RSA is homomorphic: E(m1) * E(m2) = m1^e * m2^e = (m1 * m2)^e = E(m1 * m2) mod n. An attacker can manipulate ciphertexts without knowing the plaintexts. For example, if the attacker intercepts c = E(m) and wants to learn m, they can compute c' = c * r^e mod n = E(m * r) for a known random r, submit c' for decryption (chosen-ciphertext attack), receive m * r, and compute m = (m * r) * r^(-1) mod n. Prevention by OAEP: OAEP includes a structured padding with hash-based integrity checks. After decryption of a manipulated ciphertext, the OAEP padding check fails (the hash does not match), and the decryption returns an error rather than the manipulated plaintext. This makes chosen-ciphertext attacks impossible because the attacker cannot construct a valid OAEP-padded ciphertext from a manipulated one.

rubric.key: Correct description of deterministic encryption attack with example
Correct description of homomorphism/CCA attack with specific exploitation
Clear explanation of how OAEP prevents each attack

questions.question: A 256-bit ECC key provides approximately 128 bits of security, while a 3072-bit RSA key also provides approximately 128 bits of security. Explain mathematically why ECC achieves the same security level with a much smaller key, referencing the best known attacks against each system.
questions.answer: The difference is in the computational complexity of the best known attacks against each mathematical problem. RSA (integer factorization): The best known classical algorithm for factoring n-bit integers is the General Number Field Sieve, with complexity approximately L_n[1/3, 1.923] = exp(1.923 * (ln n)^(1/3) * (ln ln n)^(2/3)). This is sub-exponential: faster than exponential but slower than polynomial. For 3072-bit RSA, GNFS requires approximately 2^128 operations. Because the attack is sub-exponential, achieving more security requires disproportionately larger keys: doubling the security level requires more than doubling the key size. ECC (elliptic curve discrete logarithm): The best known classical algorithm for ECDLP is Pollard's rho, with complexity O(sqrt(#E)) where #E is approximately 2^k for k-bit keys. This is fully exponential: O(2^(k/2)). For 256-bit ECC, Pollard's rho requires approximately 2^128 operations. Because the attack is exponential, achieving more security requires only proportionally larger keys: doubling the security level doubles the key size. Mathematical comparison: at 128 bits of security, RSA needs 3072 bits (sub-exponential attack, need much larger key to compensate) while ECC needs only 256 bits (exponential attack, key size directly maps to security level). The ratio gets worse for RSA at higher security levels: 256-bit security requires 15360-bit RSA but only 512-bit ECC. This efficiency advantage makes ECC preferred for modern cryptographic applications, especially in constrained environments.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of GNFS sub-exponential complexity for RSA
Correct explanation of Pollard's rho exponential complexity for ECC
Clear mathematical reasoning for why the complexity difference leads to key size difference

questions.question: For a hash function with 160-bit output (like SHA-1): (a) how many hash computations are needed for a 50% probability of finding a collision (birthday attack), (b) how many for a pre-image attack, and (c) explain why SHA-1 is considered broken for collision resistance but not for pre-image resistance.
questions.answer: (a) Birthday attack for collision: The birthday bound for 50% collision probability is approximately sqrt(pi/2 * N) = 1.177 * sqrt(N) where N = 2^160. Number of hashes = 1.177 * 2^80 = approximately 2^80. So approximately 2^80 hash computations are needed for a 50% probability of finding a collision. (b) Pre-image attack: Finding m such that H(m) = h for a given h requires approximately N/2 = 2^160/2 = 2^159 hash computations on average (half the space by expected value), or 2^160 for near-certainty. (c) Why SHA-1 is broken for collisions but not pre-images: SHA-1's collision resistance is broken because researchers (Wang et al., 2005) found analytical techniques that exploit the internal structure of SHA-1's compression function to find collisions in approximately 2^63 operations, far less than the 2^80 birthday bound. Google demonstrated a practical collision (SHAttered) in 2017 using approximately 2^63.1 SHA-1 computations. However, pre-image resistance requires a fundamentally different attack: recovering the input from the output. The known attacks on SHA-1 exploit differential path analysis in the compression function to find two inputs with the same output, but these techniques do not help find a specific input that produces a given output. The best known pre-image attack on SHA-1 has complexity approximately 2^159.3, which is negligibly better than brute force (2^160) and completely infeasible. The distinction: collision attacks exploit the birthday paradox structure (finding any pair) and internal differential analysis. Pre-image attacks require inverting the hash, which is a harder problem not aided by these techniques.

rubric.key: Correct birthday bound calculation yielding approximately 2^80
Correct pre-image complexity of approximately 2^160
Clear explanation of why collision attacks succeed while pre-image attacks do not, referencing differential analysis

questions.question: The Debian OpenSSL bug (2008) reduced the entropy of generated RSA keys to approximately 15 bits. Calculate how many possible unique 1024-bit RSA key pairs could be generated by affected systems, and estimate the time needed to enumerate all of them at 1000 key generations per second.
questions.answer: The Debian OpenSSL bug initialized the PRNG seed using only the process ID (PID). On Linux, PIDs range from 1 to 32768 (2^15 = 32768 values). Since the PRNG is deterministic given its seed, each PID produces a deterministic sequence of random numbers, and therefore a deterministic RSA key pair for each key size and architecture. Number of possible unique 1024-bit RSA key pairs: Approximately 32,768 (one per PID) per architecture (32-bit vs 64-bit). For a single architecture: 2^15 = 32,768 possible keys. At 1000 key generations per second: Time to enumerate all = 32,768 / 1000 = 32.8 seconds. In reality, HDMoore generated all possible keys for common key sizes and architectures and published them in a database. Any SSL certificate or SSH key generated on a Debian/Ubuntu system between September 2006 and May 2008 could be checked against this database in milliseconds. Impact: approximately 600,000 SSL certificates and millions of SSH keys were generated during the affected period. Any of these could be factored (the private key recovered) by looking up the public key in the pre-computed database. The fix required regenerating all affected keys and revoking all affected certificates. Security lesson: the entropy of the PRNG seed determines the security of every cryptographic key generated from it. Reducing entropy from 128 bits to 15 bits transforms a computationally secure system into one broken in seconds.

questions.question: A multi-factor authentication system uses: (1) an 8-character password from a 62-character alphabet, (2) a 6-digit TOTP code, and (3) a hardware security key (FIDO2). If an attacker has stolen the password hash (and can attempt offline cracking at 10 billion hashes/second) but does not have the TOTP secret or the hardware key, analyze the effective security of each factor and the combined system.
questions.answer: Factor 1 - Password (offline attack with stolen hash): Key space = 62^8 = 2.18 x 10^14 possible passwords. At 10 billion (10^10) hashes/second: time to exhaust = 2.18 x 10^14 / 10^10 = 21,800 seconds = approximately 6 hours. Expected time (average) = approximately 3 hours. With bcrypt (cost 12, approximately 4 hashes/second): 2.18 x 10^14 / 4 = 5.45 x 10^13 seconds = approximately 1.7 million years. Assessment: weak with fast hashes, strong with bcrypt. Factor 2 - TOTP (online attack only, since the secret is not stolen): Key space = 10^6 = 1,000,000 per 30-second window. With typical rate limiting (3 attempts per window): P(success per window) = 3/10^6 = 0.0003%. Expected windows to success = 333,333 windows = approximately 116 days of continuous attempts. Account lockout after N failures would prevent this entirely. Assessment: strong against online attack, impossible offline without the TOTP secret. Factor 3 - Hardware security key (FIDO2): Cannot be brute-forced. The private key never leaves the hardware token. Authentication requires physical possession and user presence verification. The attacker must physically steal the hardware key. Assessment: provides the strongest factor; resistant to all remote attacks. Combined system: The attacker must defeat ALL three factors. Having cracked the password (Factor 1), they still need both the TOTP code and physical possession of the hardware key. The TOTP requires either stealing the TOTP secret (requires access to the authentication server or the user's TOTP app) or performing an online guessing attack (116+ days, detectable). The hardware key requires physical theft. The combined security is multiplicative for independent factors, but the practical security is determined by the weakest path: if the attacker can phish all three factors simultaneously (real-time phishing proxy), MFA can be defeated. FIDO2 hardware keys resist phishing because they verify the origin domain, making them the most critical factor.

rubric.key: Correct analysis of password security with and without key stretching
Correct analysis of TOTP security with rate limiting
Understanding that FIDO2 provides the strongest factor and recognition of phishing limitations of non-FIDO MFA

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction. Mathematical concepts build cumulatively and missing sessions creates gaps.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem set discussion is encouraged. All proofs, implementations, and written analyses must be individually authored. Cite any references or discussions that influenced your work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Mathematical proofs must be your own work. Using computer algebra systems (sympy, SageMath) for computation is permitted and encouraged; using them to generate proofs is not.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with Python 3, NumPy, SciPy, sympy, and matplotlib. Scientific calculators are permitted during exams. Programmable calculators and internet-connected devices are not permitted during exams.
